original_abstract: The vast chemical diversity of natural antioxidants complicates the isolation, detection, and quantification of individual compounds within complex food and biological matrices. Furthermore, evaluating total antioxidant capacity is often more biologically relevant than measuring single species, as the health-promoting effects of these compounds typically arise from their synergistic interactions. Despite this, the lack of standardized quantification protocols means that no single assay is currently universally accepted for food labeling. Existing methodologies are generally categorized into electron transfer (ET) and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanisms; however, results between these assays are rarely comparable due to variations in redox potentials, pH sensitivity, and solvent dependencies. This project aims to bridge these gaps by systematically characterizing the properties and mutual interactions of antioxidants. By establishing a more rational framework for classifying assays and addressing their inherent constraints, this work strives to enhance the comparability and clarity of antioxidant measurements. To achieve this, the task group synthesizes diverse expert experiences across multiple analytical platforms to provide a unified perspective on the field.
humanized_abstract: The enormous chemical diversity of antioxidants in plants and other living organisms makes their isolation, identification and quantification in foods and biological systems highly challenging. Moreover, it is often more relevant to measure the total antioxidant capacity of a sample, food or living organism since their beneficial health-related activities are generally the result of their synergistic action. Due to the lack of generally accepted methods for the quantitative determination of antioxidants for food labelling purposes, food analyses presently rely on an array of experimental methods, which differ depending on whether they measure antioxidant activity based on an electron transfer (ET) or hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanism. The individual measurements are however not highly inter-correlated because of the differences in redox potential, pH dependence, or requirement of different media and conditions. This project will therefore look at an in-depth characterisation of the antioxidant itself and at its mutual interactions and, by developing a more logical classification of the antioxidant assays with an account of their intrinsic limitations, with the aim of providing more cohesion and clarity in the present plethora of tests.

Due to the lack of generally accepted methods for the quantitative determination of antioxidants for food labelling purposes, food analyses presently rely on an array of experimental methods, which differ depending on whether they measure antioxidant activity based on an electron transfer (ET) or hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanism.
